REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)]. REPORT 102: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 Network-Share Failure This community centers on SAP-originated printing that relied on two named print-server VMs (VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A Windows update followed by a VM restore sequence is implicated in causing the print servers' SMB/file-share presentation of printer queues to malfunction, which blocked SAP printing until administrators disabled and re-enabled the affected network shares and printer queues to clear pending jobs and restore service. Normal/non-SAP printing continued via a separate follow-me printing path on other print servers, indicating the outage was scoped to the SAP print-path servers. Employee 20 participated in troubleshooting to determine whether the issue was on the SAP side. [Data: Entities (521,522,527,523,524); Relationships (883,887,890,891,892)] ## Primary failure mode: print-server SMB/network share malfunction after Windows update and VM restore The incident root sequence identifies a Windows update followed by a virtual-machine restore as part of the causal chain that led to network-share problems on the print-server VMs [Data: Relationships (890); Entities (523)]. The named print server VRINFRGERPRNT03 is explicitly recorded as having its printer-queue network share malfunction after the update/restore, and administrators cleared the problem by disabling and re-enabling the share on affected queues; VRINFRGERPRNT04 is also listed as unreachable for SAP printing during the incident [Data: Relationships (883,887); Entities (521,522)]. ## Business impact: SAP printing blocked until shares/queues were restored SAP is the originating application for the affected jobs and printing from SAP was blocked while the print-server shares/queues were malfunctioning, indicating direct business-process impact to SAP print-dependent workflows [Data: Entities (527); Relationships (883,887)]. Pending documents accumulated on the configured printer queues and were held until administrators disabled and re-enabled the network share for those queues, after which printing resumed—showing the outage directly caused job backlog and delivery delay for SAP print output [Data: Entities (525); Relationships (891)]. ## Scope was limited to SAP path print servers; follow-me printing and other servers unaffected The incident affected the print servers in the SAP print path (VRINFRGERPRNT03/04) while a separate follow-me printing solution continued to provide normal non-SAP printing via other print servers, indicating the outage was scoped rather than tenant-wide printing disruption [Data: Entities (526,528); Relationships (892); Entities (521,522)]. This isolation suggests different print infrastructures (SAP-targeted VMs vs. follow-me servers) and/or differing configuration/state that made only the SAP print servers susceptible to the update/restore side-effect [Data: Entities (532)]. ## Operational remediation steps were manual and effective but ad-hoc Administrators resolved the immediate blockage by disabling and re-enabling the network share exposing printer queues, which cleared pending documents and allowed printing to resume—this established an effective remediation but is a manual, reactive action tied to operator intervention [Data: Relationships (891); Entities (521)]. Employee 20 from the support team was engaged to investigate whether the cause originated on the SAP side (examining SAP-to-printer interactions), showing cross-team troubleshooting activity during the incident and that ownership boundaries were exercised in the response [Data: Entities (517,527); Relationships (906,879)]. ## Configuration and exposure: printer network share (SMB) is a key dependency and potential risk point The printer network share is specifically described as the SMB/file-share exposure used to present printer queues to clients and SAP; the share's malfunction was the immediate failure surface that blocked jobs, highlighting the share as a critical dependency in the SAP print flow [Data: Entities (524); Relationships (883,891)]. Because SMB/file-share presentation is central to the workflow, changes to VM state, OS updates, or restore processes that affect share registration or permissions can directly impact service availability, making this a high-leverage control point for both operations and security reviews [Data: Entities (524,523)]. ## Change-control and restore process gap implied by update + VM-restore linkage The timeline links a Windows update and an associated VM restore to the onset of the network-share problem, implying the update/restore process — and its post-restore verification steps — may lack sufficient validation for share/service availability after a restore [Data: Relationships (890); Entities (523)]. This pattern indicates a procedural or tooling gap in post-update/restore health checks for print-server services (SMB shares, queue registration) that should be addressed to prevent recurrence [Data: Relationships (883,891)]. ## People and communication: cross-team troubleshooting performed but formal runbook evidence not shown Records show Employee 20 was tasked to validate whether the issue was on SAP's side and was acknowledged in internal thanks from Employee 19, demonstrating collaborative troubleshooting across application and infrastructure teams during the incident [Data: Entities (517); Relationships (906,879)]. However, the documented fix (disable/re-enable of shares) appears to have been executed as an operational workaround rather than from a recorded, automated runbook in the dataset; the absence of a referenced runbook or automated remediation suggests opportunity to codify the steps for faster, lower-risk recovery in future events [Data: Relationships (891); Entities (521)].

REPORT 28: # Train/Toolbox ADFS MFA Integration (New ADFS Client) — Production Authentication Failures The community centers on an ADFS-based authentication integration for the Train system where the Toolbox client relied on a newly registered ADFS client to perform MFA requests. The new ADFS client registration contained two parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) set in a way that changed token format (JWT issuance) and delegated MFA to external claims providers; these changes broke expected authentication flows and produced reproducible failures across environments. Troubleshooting initially focused on environment changes in PROD, but investigation identified the misconfigured ADFS client registration as the root cause; remediation consisted of aligning the registration parameters with TEST and updating federation data and certificates. The investigation documents the new client, its registration object, and the problematic parameter settings as the primary configuration unit responsible for the failures. [Data: Entities (249,248,21,241,240); Relationships (390,370)] Remediation applied during the incident included adjusting the ADFS client registration parameters to match the TEST environment, updating federation data, removing old certificates, and verifying ADFS configuration — actions that directly addressed the misconfiguration and resolved the observed failures. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## Toolbox/Train MFA flows depended on the new client and reproduced failures across environments The Toolbox client on the Train system used the new ADFS-registered client specifically to perform MFA requests; since the client registration changed both token type and MFA handling, Toolbox sign-in flows were disrupted. [Data: Entities (237,249,95); Relationships (370,372)] Investigators initially assessed ADFS and considered excluding it because only PROD had been modified over the weekend, and errors reproduced across environments; however, further analysis found the registration mismatch as the root cause, underscoring the importance of configuration parity and the traceability of client registrations across environments. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65,370)] ## EnableJWT introduces token-format compatibility risk (ADFS -> JWT vs SAML expectations) EnableJWT in the ADFS client registration causes ADFS to issue JSON Web Tokens (JWTs); when downstream applications expect SAML or another token format, they may be unable to validate or consume JWTs, causing authentication failures. The records describe EnableJWT and JWT as directly linked and note that enabling JWT issuance can break downstream systems not designed to accept JWTs. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379,416,335)] Because the new client had EnableJWT enabled, this change in token type is a concrete compatibility vector that must be validated for each relying application before enabling in production; the blueprint notes deliberate selection of token format per application is required. Any rollback or permanent change should ensure token-format compatibility is validated in staging and documented in the client registration. [Data: Entities (240,242,211); Relationships (379)] ## RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA and increases operational/logging complexity The RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders parameter, when enabled, forwards MFA requirements to configured external claims providers, effectively delegating execution of MFA to those providers. That delegation alters the authentication flow and changes where and how MFA is performed, impacting traceability of MFA events. The records describe this parameter's behavior and its direct effect on MFA handling. [Data: Entities (241,95); Relationships (382,417)] Forwarding MFA to external claims providers also increases complexity for logging, monitoring, and audit trails because the system must reconcile MFA outcomes produced by external providers with internal access control logic. The analysis explicitly calls out logging and auditing implications tied to this parameter, which complicates diagnostics and compliance reporting unless telemetry and correlation are improved. [Data: Entities (243,241); Relationships (383)] ## Operational/process gap: PROD-only changes and environment parity hindered quicker identification Investigators initially focused on changes in PROD because only PROD had been modified over the weekend; the error reproducing across environments led to ADFS being initially considered but then effectively excluded at first. This indicates an operational blind spot: PROD-only changes can create cognitive bias when troubleshooting and reveal weaknesses in change-control or environment parity practices. [Data: Entities (43,21); Relationships (65)] The eventual identification of the misconfigured client registration demonstrates that configuration drift (or undocumented registration differences) between environments can mask root causes and delay remediation. Tighter deployment controls, registration promotion processes, and automated configuration comparisons between environments would reduce time-to-resolution. [Data: Entities (248,249); Relationships (390,65)] ## ADFS client registration is the primary governance control point and needs stronger lifecycle and validation controls The ADFS client registration object contains the parameters that directly control token format and MFA delegation; the incident shows that a single registration entry can change authentication semantics across an integration. The records treat the client registration as the configuration unit used to represent the new client in ADFS and as the locus for remediation. [Data: Entities (248,249,21); Relationships (390)] This implies governance actions: versioned registration templates, pre-acceptance checks for EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders, mandatory compatibility tests per relying party (SAML vs JWT), and an approval workflow for registration changes. These controls will reduce the chance that a single mis-set parameter can cause production authentication failures. [Data: Entities (248,21); Relationships (390,379,382)] ## Monitoring, auditing, and runbooks must be updated to handle MFA delegation and token-format changes Because enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders delegates MFA to external providers and enabling EnableJWT changes token format, logging and audit pipelines must be able to correlate external MFA claims and JWT issuance events back to internal sessions and access decisions. The analysis explicitly flags logging and auditing implications when MFA is forwarded to claims providers. [Data: Entities (241,243,242); Relationships (383,416)] Actionable operational changes include: adding telemetry to record when MFA is delegated, instrumenting JWT issuance events, updating SIEM parsing rules to recognize external MFA claim types and JWT indicators, and creating an incident runbook that includes steps for validating client registration parameters, token-format checks, and claims-provider health checks. These measures are required to maintain diagnostic clarity and compliance posture. [Data: Entities (243,241,240); Relationships (383,379)] ## Priority remediation and governance recommendations (actionable and ordered) Immediate actions: revert or align the problematic ADFS client registration parameters in PROD to the known-good TEST values (which was performed during the incident), validate federation data and certificate status, and confirm Toolbox/Train sign-in behavior in a controlled environment before re-enabling changes. The record of remediation includes changing the ADFS client parameters and updating federation data and certificates. [Data: Entities (21,248,249); Relationships (390,65)] Short-to-medium term governance: implement mandatory registration review and approval workflows, require token-format compatibility testing (SAML vs JWT) for each relying party, require explicit validation when enabling RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders including test coverage for external claims-provider responses, add SIEM/monitoring rules to surface unexpected token types and delegated MFA events, and codify an ADFS client registration checklist and runbook for escalations. These steps directly address the identified technical faults and operational weaknesses surfaced by the incident. [Data: Entities (248,241,240,243); Relationships (379,382,383)]

REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)]. REPORT 173: # SAP Print Servers — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 Windows‑Update Incident Two dedicated SAP print‑server VMs (VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04) failed to print SAP jobs after a maintenance window. Initial remediation focused on restarting the Windows Print Spooler and server reboots, which did not restore functionality. A rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot was performed, after which both servers exhibited blue screens (BSOD). The evidence in the corpus points to a Windows update or OS/version change as the suspected root cause; the infrastructure on‑call team was activated and a service manager/engineer (Employee 19) took over deeper log analysis and coordination. The failure pattern is consistent across multiple SAP printserver instances, indicating a systemic issue that requires controlled rollback validation, deeper OS/driver/host diagnostics, and tighter change gating and post‑change validation for print infrastructure. ## VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 are the primary affected nodes in a clustered SAP printserver deployment Two specific Windows print‑server VMs (VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04) are explicitly identified as SAP print servers implicated in the incident; both host SAP print queues and are members of the SAP PRINT SERVERS logical group. This anchors the outage to a defined, business‑critical service boundary (SAP printing) rather than an isolated workstation or single ephemeral VM [Data: Entities (154,155,545); Relationships (913,914,907)]. Because both VMs belong to the same logical SAP printserver group and showed the same failure pattern, remediation and future controls should be designed and tested at the service‑group level (SAP PRINT SERVERS) rather than treating each VM as an entirely independent asset. The records show the same troubleshooting steps and outcomes applied to both hosts, which supports a service‑level view of risk and recovery planning [Data: Entities (154,155,545); Relationships (913,914,907)]. ## Print spooler restarts were attempted but did not remediate the outage The Windows PRINT SPOOLER service was explicitly restarted on the affected machines as an initial remediation action, but this did not restore SAP printing functionality, indicating the root cause is likely below or outside of simple spooler process failure (for example OS, drivers, or a Windows update interaction) [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (907,914,922)]. The operational implication is that runbooks that treat spooler restarts as a primary or sufficient first‑line fix for SAP printserver faults are incomplete for this failure class. Playbooks should capture escalation criteria when spooler restarts fail (for example: collect event logs, suspend further changes, escalate to infrastructure on‑call) to avoid wasted cycles and to accelerate diagnostics [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (907,914,922)]. ## A Windows update / OS version change is the leading suspected root cause and rollback was requested The corpus records an explicit suspicion that a Windows update or Windows version change impacted print functionality and documents a request to roll back the Windows version on affected print servers; the infrastructure on‑call also documented suspicion that a Windows update affected printing, and restores to a pre‑maintenance snapshot were performed as a remedial action [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (910)]. This attribution to Windows updates changes the investigative focus toward update/patch levels, driver compatibility, and recent kernel or print‑related fixes. It also creates an immediate governance action: pauses or holds on further OS updates for the SAP printserver cohort until a controlled validation and remediation plan is established and tested [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (910)]. ## Restores produced blue screens (BSOD) indicating OS/driver/host‑level instability beyond simple spooler faults After the rollback/restore to the pre‑maintenance snapshot, affected servers experienced blue screens (BSOD), which were reported by contact personnel. The presence of BSODs after restore shows the fault is not limited to a transient spooler state and suggests kernel‑level, driver, or VM host/compatibility issues requiring deeper forensic diagnostics (memory dumps, driver lists, host hypervisor logs) [Data: Entities (542,154,155); Relationships (911)]. BSODs materially increase incident severity because they obstruct in‑place troubleshooting and imply potential corruption or incompatibility at the OS or hypervisor layer. Recovery will likely require coordinated work across server OS, virtualization/host, and driver teams and careful post‑restore validation in a controlled environment before returning to production [Data: Entities (542,154,155); Relationships (911)]. ## Infrastructure on‑call was activated and a Service Manager performed deeper analysis and cross‑team coordination The infrastructure ON‑CALL team was specifically activated to restore VRINFRGERPRNT03 to its pre‑maintenance state and coordinate recovery actions, indicating recognized operational escalation to a higher response tier [Data: Entities (538); Relationships (908)]. Additionally, EMPLOYEE 19, a Service Manager who also conducted hands‑on engineering work and further troubleshooting using event/log data, was asked to take over analysis of the BSOD and printing issues and communicated gratitude/recognition internally. This documents clear human ownership for post‑restore analysis and provides a named contact for follow‑up, lessons learned, and runbook updates [Data: Entities (516); Relationships (879,908)]. ## Identical failure pattern across multiple SAP printserver VMs indicates systemic risk and recurrence potential Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited the same failure progression: print failure → spooler/service restarts → rollback to snapshot → blue screens during post‑restore testing. The repeated pattern across multiple hosts strongly suggests a systemic trigger (for example a pushed OS update, shared driver package, or hypervisor change) rather than random independent hardware faults [Data: Entities (154,155); Relationships (914,911,910)]. Special focus is placed on the consistency of printer queue configuration (Queue Properties) and network sharing and publishing mechanisms, as these may be relevant for SAP print paths and spool processing. Typical symptoms of deviations from this standard include: print jobs remaining stuck in SAP, print jobs disappearing from the queue without printing, or print jobs accumulating on the print server and only being released after manual intervention. This pattern was observed in ticket TI-931293 after a Windows update and subsequent VM restore. design Architecture overview SAP System → SAP Spool / Output Management → SAP print target (logical printer definition) → Windows Printserver (SAP Printserver) → Print queues → Physical printers Roles and components 1. SAP Output Management Generates print jobs and transfers them to defined print targets. The availability and correct processing of print targets is business-critical. 2. SAP Printserver (Windows) Serves as the central processing instance for SAP printing. The relevant queues are operated on dedicated print servers (in the ticket: VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). 3. Print queues Queue properties must be standardized and consistent. After system changes (updates, restores), it must be ensured that publishing/sharing parameters and queue status are correct and that no unintended states (e.g., stuck jobs) exist. 4. Print Spooler Service The service is responsible for processing print jobs. A restart may temporarily influence symptoms but does not necessarily resolve configuration deviations at queue level. Operational principles • After Windows updates or VM restores, a validation of the SAP print servers must be performed (queue status, spooler, sharing/publishing of queues, test print from SAP). • SAP print paths must be treated as business-critical; disruptions are to be handled with priority (classified as PRIO HIGH in the ticket). • If printing issues occur after update/restore, queue configuration must be treated as the primary checkpoint, not only the spooler service. decision tree & use cases Decision Tree Initial question: Does the issue affect only printing from SAP or also normal printing (e.g., Office)? A) Only SAP printing affected, normal printing works A1: Are all SAP print targets affected or only specific print servers? • Only specific print servers affected → Perform SAP printserver-specific checks (queue status, publishing/sharing, spooler, restore state) • All print servers affected → Check SAP output configuration (print targets, spool queue, SAP-side errors) A2: Was there a Windows update or VM restore within the last 24–72 hours? • Yes → Perform post-change validation according to standard (queue properties, sharing/publishing, test print) • No → Proceed to A3 A3: Are SAP print jobs visible on the print server (queue) or do they remain stuck in SAP? • Remain stuck in SAP → Check SAP output / connection to print target • Visible on the print server but no print output → Check queue/spooler/sharing mechanism B) SAP printing and normal printing affected B1: Is the Print Spooler service running? • No → Start service / check service recovery • Yes → Proceed to B2 B2: Are queues paused or are jobs permanently stuck? • Yes → Correct queue status, clear stuck jobs, then perform test print • No → Check network path / printer reachability C) Issue occurs after update/restore and is printserver-specific C1: Check queue properties according to standard • Validate publishing/sharing status of printer queues • If inconsistent state: disable/enable relevant queue parameters as a reset measure This procedure corresponds to the pattern documented in the ticket, where after update and restore the network sharing/queue functionality had to be corrected. REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. REPORT 16: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 (TI-931293) The community centers on the SAP printing path where SAP Spool/Output Management delivers jobs to SAP Print Targets that map to dedicated Windows Printserver VMs (notably VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A high‑priority production incident (TI-931293) occurred 17–19.07.24 when SAP-generated orders could not be printed, blocking deliveries; the immediate remediation sequence included spooler restarts, a VM restore to a pre-maintenance snapshot, and disabling/re-enabling printer network shares which ultimately cleared queued jobs. The incident produced blue screens (BSOD) on the restored VMs and a customer request to rollback Windows versioning, and it exposed gaps in post-update/restore validation for queue properties, publishing/sharing parameters and end‑to‑end test prints. Multiple support engineers and the infrastructure on‑call team coordinated the recovery; the organisation has an SAP printing standard that prescribes post-change validation but the event still recurred across both SAP printserver VMs, indicating a likely server-image or Windows-update related root cause that requires deeper OS/driver/VM-host investigation and tighter runbook controls. [Data: Entities (534, 153, 154, 155, 147); Relationships (896, 913, 908, 232)] ## Production-impacting outage: SAP print jobs blocked, business deliveries affected The incident was recorded as a high‑priority printing outage affecting SAP-originated orders and deliveries, indicating direct business impact and the need for fast remediation and coordinated operations response. The ticket TI-931293 documents the inability to print from SAP and the classification and remediation timeline across 17–19.07.24. [Data: Entities (534); Relationships (896)]. Support actions were escalated to infrastructure on‑call and multiple engineers were assigned to the case, showing cross-shift coordination and handover requirements for production restores and follow-up testing (information sent to early shift for next‑day validation). This highlights both the operational severity and that the incident required multi-role participation to resolve. [Data: Relationships (905, 908)]. ## Probable root‑cause chain: Windows update -> VM restore -> network share/queue publishing corruption Documents and timelines link a Windows update and subsequent VM restore to the onset of queue-sharing and printing failures: a Windows update was applied, the print-server VMs were restored to a prior snapshot, and after the restore the printer network shares and queue publishing were malfunctioning, causing jobs to remain stuck until shares were toggled. This sequence is central to the incident's root-cause hypothesis and was specifically recorded in the log entries. [Data: Entities (537, 523); Relationships (890, 232, 883, 891)]. Contact Person 11 explicitly requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, reinforcing the operational suspicion that the Windows update/version was implicated; the infrastructure on‑call also documented this suspicion. The appearance of BSODs after the restore further suggests OS/driver/host-level instability that must be investigated beyond application-level spooler troubleshooting. [Data: Relationships (923, 911); Entities (542)]. ## Failure manifested in both SAP printserver VMs but not in follow‑me print infrastructure Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited identical failure patterns (inability to process SAP jobs, failed spooler restarts, and BSODs after restore), while other print servers supporting the follow‑me solution continued to print normally. This scoped the incident to the SAP-designated printserver VMs rather than a tenant-wide network or SAP-side-only problem. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 535); Relationships (913, 901, 887)]. Because two independent VMs in the same role failed similarly after the same maintenance/change sequence, a systemic image, driver set, or host-level change (or a common post-restore validation omission) is a likely common-mode cause that requires coordinated forensic analysis across both VM instances and their hypervisor/backup/restore processes. [Data: Relationships (890, 232)]. ## Operational runbook/validation gaps: post-update/restore checks were insufficient or incomplete An approved 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard exists and mandates post-update/restore validation steps (queue status, spooler health, publishing/sharing parameters and test prints). The incident narrative cites VM restore and Windows update as triggers requiring these checks, but the persistent failure and need to manually toggle network shares indicate either incomplete adherence to the standard or that the current validation checklist does not detect the specific failure mode observed. [Data: Entities (147, 161); Relationships (243, 246, 251)]. This demonstrates both the presence of governance (a standard) and a procedural gap: either the runbook was not fully executed during the restoration window or the runbook lacks items (e.g., explicit verification of network-share registration and directory publishing state after restore) that would have detected this issue before business impact. The standard should be revised to explicitly include steps that caught this failure in TI-931293. [Data: Relationships (251, 891)]. ## Troubleshooting sequence and stakeholder actions were appropriate but reveal handover and evidence-capture weaknesses Employee 23 performed early remediation actions (gpupdate /force on an affected workstation, spooler restart attempts, and remote troubleshooting) and then activated infrastructure on‑call; the infrastructure on‑call performed the VM restore with support from a technician (Mister X) and restoration contexts (Inter12203/Inter12204). Multiple engineers later investigated BSODs and log evidence. These documented steps show correct escalation and involvement of required roles. [Data: Entities (49, 538, 541, 539, 540); Relationships (74, 921, 908, 924, 925)]. However, the fact that BSODs and the network-share corruption occurred after the restore suggests that pre‑ and post-restore evidence capture (detailed event/log snapshots, hypervisor logs, driver versions) and controlled validation were either not available or not sufficient to prevent or immediately diagnose the failure — an evidence-capture checklist and mandatory pre-remediation snapshot of key logs should be formalised. [Data: Relationships (911, 890)]. ## Security and availability exposures from share toggling and manual fixes Administrators cleared stuck jobs and restored printing by disabling and then re-enabling the printer network shares on the affected servers. While effective in this incident, this manual share manipulation is an operationally risky fix if done without change control because it temporarily alters SMB/print sharing state and could impact visibility or cause job loss if not coordinated and audited. The logs show that the printer network share was the primary locus of the problem and the point of manual remediation. [Data: Relationships (883, 891); Entities (524)]. Recommendation: treat share toggles as controlled mitigation steps in runbooks with explicit rollback/validation and audit logging to prevent accidental exposure or data loss. Consider automating share/publishing verification as part of post-update validation to avoid manual toggles except under controlled emergency procedures. [Data: Relationships (251, 246)]. ## Recommended immediate and medium-term actions (prioritised) Immediate (P0/P1) actions: 1) Isolate a restored printserver VM to collect memory dumps, Windows event logs, spooler logs and hypervisor host logs for the restore window to determine BSOD causes; 2) if not already done, roll back to the previously known-good Windows image/version on a non-production test copy to reproduce the failure before applying tenant-wide rollbacks; 3) add mandatory post-restore checklist enforcement (validate queue properties, publishing/sharing flags, directory publishing and run an SAP-originated test print) before returning servers to production. These map to items recorded in the standard and the ticket actions. [Data: Entities (147, 523, 537); Relationships (243, 246, 923)]. Medium-term actions: 1) Augment the 'Print Services for SAP Output' standard with explicit post-restore checks for network-share registration and add automated probes that verify queue visibility from SAP and a test workstation; 2) implement a pre/post-restore automated verification job that runs spooler/queue/publishing checks and preserves logs for forensics; 3) perform a controlled update/rollback plan for Windows versions across SAP printserver VMs (staged on a test VM, with clear rollback criteria). These changes will reduce recurrence and shorten mean time to resolution for similar future incidents. [Data: Entities (147, 161, 523); Relationships (890, 891)]. ## Communication, ownership and escalation matrix to formalise The incident involved CRM/web ticket logging, a primary early responder (Employee 23), escalation to infrastructure on‑call, handover to early shift for validation, and involvement of additional engineers (Employees 19 and 20) for BSOD and SAP-side checks. This shows a functioning ad-hoc escalation chain but also highlights the need for a formal matrix documenting responsibilities for: immediate remediation (spooler/queue checks), VM restore/host interactions, OS-level diagnostics, SAP-side validation, and customer communication/approvals. [Data: Entities (49, 516, 517, 538, 543); Relationships (74, 921, 905, 879, 906)]. Create an escalation runbook that lists required evidence to collect at each handover (logs, timestamps, snapshots), decision criteria for rollback vs. rebuild, and a named on-call roster to reduce ambiguity in future incidents. Ensure the runbook maps to the approved standard and is practiced in tabletop exercises. [Data: Entities (147); Relationships (905)].
